When we assess the arithmetical validity of this argument, we assume that the numerals ‘1’, ‘2’ and ‘8’ refer to the numbers 1, 2 and 8, respectively; that the symbol ‘+’ denotes the operation of addition; and that the symbols ‘=’, ‘<’ and ‘>’ stand for the relations of equality, being less than, and being greater than, respectively. These are the logical elements of the language appearing in the argument. The non-logical elements of the language are the variables \(x\) and \(y\), which may stand for any numbers. The argument is valid just in case no matter what numbers the variables \(x\) and \(y\) stand for: if it is true that \(x+2y=-2\) and \(y>1\), then it is also true that \(8<-2x\).
Of course, if one were charged with the task of assessing the validity of this argument—say, in an algebra class—one would most certainly not proceed by substituting in particular numerical values for \(x\) and \(y\) in an effort to ascertain whether or not there exists a particular interpretation of these variables under which the premises of the argument are true and its conclusion false. For one, there are, infinitely many such interpretations. Instead, to assess this argument’s validity, a more feasible approach would be to attempt to prove the conclusion from the premises using the basic rules of algebra.
In the typical language in which algebraic derivations are expressed, a proof of this argument’s validity could take the following form:

Proof. Since \(y>1\), it follows that \(2y>2\) and therefore that \(x+2y>x+2\). But since \(x+2y=-2\), we have:
\[
  - 2 > x + 2
\]
Subtracting \(2\) from both sides of the above inequality gives us:
\[
  - 4 > x
\]
Multiplying both sides of this inequality by \(-2\) yields \(8 < - 2x\), which is the desired conclusion.

Taking this example as our model, when we speak of a method of reasoning, we will always have in mind methods which employ as a standard for argumentative validity the existence of a proof of an argument’s conclusion from its premises. In order to make this idea precise, we must say more about what we mean by a proof.
Let us consider again the above algebraic derivation. In this proof, we start with one of the premises, namely \(y > 1\). We then transform this premise into another sentence, \(2y > 2\), by applying a certain rule of algebra, namely the rule which allows us to multiply both sides of an inequality by any positive number. We might transcribe this transformation as follows:


\(1\)
\(y > 1\)
Premise


\(2\)
\(2\cdot y > 2\)
Multiply by 2: \(1\)


The note at the end of the last line indicates that we are applying the rule “Multiply by 2” to line 1. This rule can be formulated as follows:
(Multiply by 2) For any numerical expressions \(\alpha\) and \(\beta\): from \(\alpha> \beta\), it follows that \(2\cdot \alpha> 2\cdot \beta\).
Note, however, that if we are strict about how we apply this rule, it doesn’t quite get us what we want. For, if we apply the rule, as stated, to the sentence \(y > 1\), what we actually get is \(2\cdot y>2\cdot 1\)—not \(2\cdot y > 2\). Now, of course, we all know it to be a basic fact about arithmetic that \(2\cdot 1 = 2\), and so we naturally perform the requisite substitution in our head. But to give a more complete transcription of the reasoning here, we ought to be explicit and include the substition as another step in the proof:


\(1\)
\(y > 1\)
Premise


\(2\)
\(2\cdot y > 2\cdot 1\)
Multiply by 2: \(1\)


\(3\)
\(2\cdot 1 = 2\)
Arithmetic


\(4\)
\(2\cdot y > 2\)
Substitution of Equals: \(2, 3\)


Here we appeal to the basic arithmetical fact that \(2\cdot 1 = 2\) in line 3 of the proof. A rule of substitution is then applied to lines 2 and 3 of the proof to give us line 4. This substitution rule can be stated as follows:
(Substitution of Equals) Let \(\psi\) be the result of substituting one numerical expression \(\beta\) for another numerical expression \(\alpha\) in the sentence \(\varphi\). Then, from \(\alpha=\beta\) and \(\varphi\), it follows that \(\psi\).
While the proof is now correct, the rule Multiply by 2 seems a bit too specific. After all, we don’t want to have to posit separate rules for every possible number that we might want to utilize as a multiplier in an algebraic proof. A better rule, that licenses the general sort of transformation that we have in mind, would be something like:
(Multiply by \(n>0\)) For any number \(n\), and any numerical expressions \(\alpha\) and \(\beta\): from \(n > 0\) and \(\alpha> \beta\), it follows that \(n\cdot \alpha> n\cdot \beta\).
To apply this rule, we now have to cite a further arithmetical fact, namely, that \(2 >
0\). All told, now, the proof looks like this:


\(1\)
\(y > 1\)
Premise


\(2\)
\(2 > 0\)
Arithmetic


\(3\)
\(2\cdot y > 2\cdot 1\)
Multiply by \(n > 0\): \(1, 2\)


\(4\)
\(2\cdot 1 = 2\)
Arithmetic


\(5\)
\(2\cdot y > 2\)
Substitution of Equals: \(3, 4\)


This, of course, is only the first step of the reasoning. But if we continue in this way, spelling out the proof in detail, one step at a time, eventually the reasoning will terminate with the conclusion of the argument appearing as the last line of the proof.


\(1\)
\(y > 1\)
Premise


\(2\)
\(2 > 0\)
Arithmetic


\(3\)
\(2\cdot y > 2\cdot 1\)
Multiply by \(n > 0\): \(1, 2\)


\(4\)
\(2\cdot 1 = 2\)
Arithmetic


\(5\)
\(2\cdot y > 2\)
Substitution of Equals: \(3, 4\)


\[\vdots\]




\(n\)
\(8 < - 2\cdot x\)
Some Rule: \(?, \ldots, ?\)



Exercise 1.12 Complete the arithmetical proof started above. Add any additional rules that are required to deduce the conclusion from the premises together with basic arithmetical facts.

We will take this expanded arithmetical derivation as a model for the simplest sort of proofs that we will consider in the context of our formal languages (shortly we will consider other kinds of proofs with a slightly more complicated structure).
What is important to note about proofs such as this is that all of the claims that are asserted in the course of the reasoning fall into one of the following three categories:

Premises of the argument (e.g., \(y > 1\)).
Basic facts of arithmetic (e.g., \(2>0\) or \(2\cdot 1 = 2\)).
The result of applying a rule of inference to claims already established in the proof.

In general, we refer to the sort of basic claims that we can freely appeal to in the course of giving a proof as axioms. With respect to a set of axioms and rules of inference, each of which tells us that certain sentences follow from others, a proof can now be defined as follows:

Definition 1.7 Given a set of axioms and rules of inference, a proof of \(\varphi\) from \(\Gamma\) is a finite sequence of sentences, the last of which is the sentence \(\varphi\) and each of which is either (1) a member of the set \(\Gamma\); (2) an axiom; or (3) the result of applying one of the rules of inference to sentences appearing earlier in the proof.

Ideally, the system of axioms and rules in which we give proofs should ensure that the following is true: an argument is valid just in case there exists a proof of its conclusion from its premises. In the following section, we will introduce such a proof system for the formal language L\(_S\).

Exercise 1.13  

Does there exist a proof of \(\varphi\) from the set of sentences \(\Gamma\cup\{\varphi\}\)? Explain.
If there exists a proof of \(\varphi\) from the set of sentences \(\Gamma\), does there exist a proof of \(\varphi\) from \(\Gamma\cup\Delta\)? Explain.
Show that if there exists a proof of \(\varphi\) from \(\Gamma\), then there exists a proof of \(\varphi\) from \(\Gamma\) in which no sentence appears more than once.




1.5.2 Proofs in L\(_S\)
The history of formal logic begins with Aristotle’s theory of the syllogism. In what is perhaps the first treatise on the subject of formal logic, the Prior Analytics, Aristotle draws a distinction between valid arguments which he regards as ‘perfect’ and those which he regards as imperfect. While the validity of perfect arguments is self-evident and requires no further justification, the validity of imperfect arguments stands in need of proof. To provide such proofs, Aristotle introduces a method of reasoning by which imperfectly valid arguments can be derived by means of certain rules of inference.
In the remainder of this chapter, we will present a version of Aristotle’s proof system for the formal language L\(_S\). This system includes both axioms and rules of inference by which certain sentences can be inferred from others.6
The axioms of the system are all sentences of the form:
\[
A\textrm{\textbf{a}}A
\]
where \(A\) is any term. The basic rules of inference are as follows:7
(Barbara):
\[
\frac{A\textrm{\textbf{a}}B\quad,\quad B\textrm{\textbf{a}}C}{A\textrm{\textbf{a}}C}
\]
(Celarent):
\[
\frac{A\textrm{\textbf{a}}B\quad,\quad B\textrm{\textbf{e}}C}{A\textrm{\textbf{e}}C}
\]
(e-conversion):
\[
\frac{A\textrm{\textbf{e}}B}{B\textrm{\textbf{e}}A}
\]
By means of these axioms and rules, we can give proofs to establish the validity of arguments in L\(_S\). For example, we can show that the following argument is valid:
\[
A\textrm{\textbf{a}}B, B\textrm{\textbf{a}}C, D\textrm{\textbf{e}}C \vdash D\textrm{\textbf{e}}A
\]
The proof is as follows:


\(1\)
\(A\textrm{\textbf{a}}B\)
Premise


\(2\)
\(B\textrm{\textbf{a}}C\)
Premise


\(3\)
\(A\textrm{\textbf{a}}C\)
Barbara: \(1, 2\)


\(4\)
\(D\textrm{\textbf{e}}C\)
Premise


\(5\)
\(C\textrm{\textbf{e}}D\)
e-conversion: \(4\)


\(6\)
\(A\textrm{\textbf{e}}D\)
Celarent: \(3, 5\)


\(7\)
\(D\textrm{\textbf{e}}A\)
e-conversion: \(6\)


Here’s another example:
\[
A\textrm{\textbf{a}}B, B\textrm{\textbf{e}}C, D\textrm{\textbf{a}}A, E\textrm{\textbf{a}}D \vdash C\textrm{\textbf{e}}E
\]
the proof is:


\(1\)
\(A\textrm{\textbf{a}}B\)
Premise


\(2\)
\(B\textrm{\textbf{e}}C\)
Premise


\(3\)
\(A\textrm{\textbf{e}}C\)
Celarent: \(1, 2\)


\(4\)
\(D\textrm{\textbf{a}}A\)
Premise


\(5\)
\(D\textrm{\textbf{e}}C\)
Celarent: \(3, 4\)


\(6\)
\(E\textrm{\textbf{a}}D\)
Premise


\(7\)
\(E\textrm{\textbf{e}}C\)
Celarent: \(5, 6\)


\(8\)
\(C\textrm{\textbf{e}}E\)
e-conversion: \(7\)


Note that there may be other ways of proving the same argument, for example:


\(1\)
\(D\textrm{\textbf{a}}A\)
Premise


\(2\)
\(A\textrm{\textbf{a}}B\)
Premise


\(3\)
\(D\textrm{\textbf{a}}B\)
Barbara: \(1, 2\)


\(4\)
\(E\textrm{\textbf{a}}D\)
Premise


\(5\)
\(E\textrm{\textbf{a}}B\)
Barbara: \(3, 4\)


\(6\)
\(B\textrm{\textbf{e}}C\)
Premise


\(7\)
\(E\textrm{\textbf{e}}C\)
Celarent: \(5, 6\)


\(8\)
\(C\textrm{\textbf{e}}E\)
e-conversion: \(7\)


While the axioms and rules of inference listed above allow us to establish the validity of a number of arguments, taken together, the resulting proof system is clearly inadequate to prove all valid arguments. For instance, this system does not allow us to prove any non-circular arguments with a conclusion that is of the form \(A\textrm{\textbf{i}}B\) or \(A\textrm{\textbf{o}}B\). For none of the axioms are of this form and none of the rules of inference license the inference of a sentence of this form from other sentences. Thus, the only way that \(A\textrm{\textbf{i}}B\) or \(A\textrm{\textbf{o}}B\) can appear in a proof, is if that sentence appears among the premises, that is, if the argument is circular. Of course, as we know, there are many valid non-circular arguments with a conclusion that is of the form \(A\textrm{\textbf{i}}B\) or \(A\textrm{\textbf{o}}B\), for example:
\[
A\textrm{\textbf{i}}B, B\textrm{\textbf{a}}C\vdash A\textrm{\textbf{i}}C
\]
To enable the proof of such arguments, we could supplement the proof system by adding more rules of inference. Indeed, Aristotle, in the Prior Analytics tells us just which rules we need to add. Interestingly, however, this is not the primary approach adopted by Aristotle. To prove such arguments, Aristotle instead employs a new method of proof which he have not yet considered, the method of reductio ad absurdum or proof by contradiction. To formalize proofs by contradiction, we have to revisit our original definition of a proof and expand it so as to accommodate what we shall refer to as ‘hypothetical’ proofs.


1.5.3 Proof by Contradiction
The proofs that we have discussed so far have all had a very simple structure. They have consisted of sequences of sentences, each of which is either an axiom, a premise, or the result of applying a rule of inference to sentences previously appearing in the proof. In such simple proofs, every claim put forward in the course of the reasoning is asserted on the grounds of its being a logical consequence of the premises. In everyday reasoning, however, it is often convenient to posit a claim which we do not take ourselves to have established—and so do not wish to assert—but which instead serves as a supposition to be utilized indirectly in some hypothetical mode of reasoning.
Consider, for example, the method of reductio ad absurdum, or proof by contradiction. When we give a proof by contradiction, we suppose that a certain claim is true for the sake of deriving a contradiction. Having done so, we then discharge our supposition and conclude that the claim that we supposed to be true is, in fact, false. In such proofs, we posit a claim and reason from it despite the fact that this claim has not been shown to follow from the premises. Instead the claim serves as a hypothesis, introduced for the specific purpose of deriving a contradiction.
In the formal language L\(_S\), the method of proof by contradiction can be implemented by appealing to the fact that for every sentence in the language L\(_S\), there exists another sentence which, on the extensional semantics introduced above, is its contradictory opposite, in the sense that the one is true, on a given interpretation, iff the other is false (see Exercise 1.10):

Definition 1.8 The contradictory of \(A\textrm{\textbf{a}}B\) is \(A\textrm{\textbf{o}}B\) and vice versa. The contradictory of \(A\textrm{\textbf{e}}B\) is \(A\textrm{\textbf{i}}B\) and vice versa. For any sentence \(\varphi\), we write \(\neg\varphi\) for the contradictory of \(\varphi\).

Note that the symbol ‘\(\neg\)’ is not in the alphabet of the language L\(_S\). We introduce it here as a metalinguistic device for referring to one sentence by means of another. Thus, for example, \(\neg A\textrm{\textbf{i}}B\) is not a new sentence of the language L\(_S\). It is simply another name for the sentence \(A\textrm{\textbf{e}}B\).
Appealing to the notion of the contradictory of a sentence, we can describe the method of proof by contradiction as follows: (1) first, posit, as a hypothesis, a sentence \(\varphi\); (2) next, derive from this hypothesis, together with the premises, a contradictory pair of sentences of the form \(\psi\) and \(\neg\psi\); (3) finally, having done so, discharge the hypothesis, and conclude that the premises entail the contradictory of the hypothesis, \(\neg\varphi\).
As an illustration of this procedure, let us see how one might try to utilize the method of proof by contradiction to establish the validity of the following argument:
\[
A\textrm{\textbf{i}}B, B\textrm{\textbf{a}}C\vdash A\textrm{\textbf{i}}C
\]
In accordance with the description of the method just given, we would like our proof system to license the following sort of reasoning:


\(1\)
\(A\textrm{\textbf{e}}C\)
Hypothesis


\(2\)
\(B\textrm{\textbf{a}}C\)
Premise


\(3\)
\(C\textrm{\textbf{e}}A\)
Premise


\(4\)
\(B\textrm{\textbf{e}}A\)
Celarent: \(2, 3\)


\(5\)
\(A\textrm{\textbf{e}}B\)
e-conversion: \(4\)


\(6\)
\(A\textrm{\textbf{i}}B\)
Premise: \(4\)


\(7\)
\(A\textrm{\textbf{i}}C\)
Contradiction: \(3, 4\)


Line 1 of this proof introduces \(A\textrm{\textbf{e}}C\) as a hypothesis; lines 2–6 deduce various consequences of this hypothesis in combination with the premises; lines 5 and 6 contain the desired contradiction; and line 7 concludes the proof by discharging the hypothesis and asserting its contradictory, which is the desired result.
This sequence of sentences, of course, is not a proof in the sense of the term specified in Definition 1.7, for \(A\textrm{\textbf{e}}C\) is neither a premise nor an axiom of the system. In the simple proofs that we have thus far considered, there is simply no mechanism for introducing a new hypothesis not included among the premises, nor for discharging that same hypothesis at some later stage of the reasoning. Consequently, there is no way of indicating that the hypothesis \(A\textrm{\textbf{e}}C\) is meant to be in effect in lines 1–6, but not in line 7.
One common notation that is often employed for indicating the introduction and subsequent discharge of hypotheses is what is sometimes called Fitch notation.8 In a Fitch-notation, proofs are not represented as simple “flat” sequences but as leveled sequences. To add a sentence to the set of hypotheses one writes down the sentence and increments the level of indentation by one. This same hypothesis is then later discharged by restoring the indentation of the proof to its previous level. Levels of indentation are explicitly marked using vertical lines and new hypotheses are highlighted by underlining them. The proof terminates with the desired conclusion appearing on the last line of the proof without indentation, signaling that all introduced hypotheses have been discharged.
In Fitch notation, the the above proof by contradiction is written:



\(1\)

\(A\textrm{\textbf{e}}C\)
Hypothesis



\(2\)

\(C\textrm{\textbf{a}}B\)
Premise



\(3\)

\(C\textrm{\textbf{e}}A\)
Premise



\(4\)

\(B\textrm{\textbf{e}}A\)
Celarent: \(2, 3\)



\(5\)

\(A\textrm{\textbf{e}}B\)
e-conversion: \(4\)



\(6\)

\(A\textrm{\textbf{i}}B\)
Premise



\(7\)
\(A\textrm{\textbf{i}}C\)
Contradiction: \(5, 6\)


This format makes it clear that the hypothesis \(A\textrm{\textbf{e}}C\) is introduced in line 1 and discharged in line 7.
Now, let’s consider the argument:
\[
D\textrm{\textbf{a}}B, A\textrm{\textbf{o}}B, A\textrm{\textbf{a}}C\vdash C\textrm{\textbf{o}}D
\]
One strategy for proving this argument is to first establish, by means of a proof by contradiction, that \(C\textrm{\textbf{o}}B\) follows from the premises \(A\textrm{\textbf{o}}B\) and \(A\textrm{\textbf{a}}C\). One could then show, again using a proof by contradiction, that \(C\textrm{\textbf{o}}D\) follows from the sentence \(C\textrm{\textbf{o}}B\) just established, and the remaining premise \(D\textrm{\textbf{a}}B\).



\(1\)

\(C\textrm{\textbf{a}}D\)
Hypothesis



\(2\)


\(C\textrm{\textbf{a}}B\)
Hypothesis



\(3\)


\(A\textrm{\textbf{a}}C\)
Premise



\(4\)


\(A\textrm{\textbf{a}}B\)
Barbara: \(2, 3\)



\(5\)


\(A\textrm{\textbf{o}}B\)
Premise



\(6\)

\(C\textrm{\textbf{o}}B\)
Contradiction: \(4, 5\)



\(7\)

\(D\textrm{\textbf{a}}B\)
Premise



\(8\)

\(C\textrm{\textbf{a}}B\)
Barbara: \(1, 7\)



\(9\)
\(C\textrm{\textbf{o}}D\)
Contradiction: \(6, 8\)


While this is a correct proof, it is does not perspicuously convey the structure of the reasoning. One of its more confusing features is that the hypothesis \(C\textrm{\textbf{a}}D\), which is the target of the second proof by contradiction (lines 1 and 6–8), is still in effect as a hypothesis in the first proof by contradiction (lines 2–5). Since \(C\textrm{\textbf{a}}D\) plays no role in the latter subproof, it seems more natural to introduce this hypothesis only after the first proof by contradiction has been completed.
This version of the proof, in which the two proofs by contradiction are done one after the other is written:



\(1\)

\(C\textrm{\textbf{a}}B\)
Hypothesis



\(2\)

\(A\textrm{\textbf{a}}C\)
Premise



\(3\)

\(A\textrm{\textbf{a}}B\)
Barbara: \(1, 2\)



\(4\)

\(A\textrm{\textbf{o}}B\)
Premise



\(5\)
\(C\textrm{\textbf{o}}B\)
Contradiction: \(3, 4\)



\(6\)

\(C\textrm{\textbf{a}}D\)
Hypothesis



\(7\)

\(D\textrm{\textbf{a}}B\)
Premise



\(8\)

\(C\textrm{\textbf{a}}B\)
Barbara: \(6, 7\)



\(9\)

\(C\textrm{\textbf{o}}B\)
Reiteration, 5



\(10\)
\(C\textrm{\textbf{o}}D\)
Contradiction: \(8, 9\)


Note that in the line 9 of this proof, we reiterate the conclusion of the first proof by contradiction appearing in line 5, inserting in into the second proof by contradiction. In general, even if the context has shifted, you are free to reiterate any line of a proof as long as the hypotheses under which it was established are all still in effect. This allows you to “bring forward” an earlier statement into the part of the proof where it is needed.
Let’s try another example:
\[
A\textrm{\textbf{a}}B, A\textrm{\textbf{o}}B\vdash C\textrm{\textbf{e}}D
\]
First, let’s confirm for ourselves that the argument is valid. Clearly there is no interpretation under which all of the premises are true and the conclusion false, since there is no interpretation under which all of the premises are true. So, the argument is indeed valid. The argument can be proven by contradiction as follows:



\(1\)

\(C\textrm{\textbf{i}}D\)
Hypothesis



\(2\)

\(A\textrm{\textbf{a}}B\)
Premise



\(3\)

\(A\textrm{\textbf{o}}B\)
Premise



\(4\)
\(C\textrm{\textbf{e}}D\)
Contradicition: \(2, 3\)



Exercise 1.14 Show that the following arguments are valid by giving a proof of the conclusion from the premises:

\(A\textrm{\textbf{i}}B\vdash B\textrm{\textbf{i}}A\)
\(A\textrm{\textbf{a}}B, B\textrm{\textbf{e}}C, C\textrm{\textbf{i}}D\vdash A\textrm{\textbf{e}}C\)
\(A\textrm{\textbf{a}}B, A\textrm{\textbf{i}}A\vdash B\textrm{\textbf{i}}A\)
\(A\textrm{\textbf{e}}B, B\textrm{\textbf{i}}A\vdash D\textrm{\textbf{o}}C\)
\(A\textrm{\textbf{i}}B, B\textrm{\textbf{e}}C\vdash A\textrm{\textbf{o}}C\)
\(A\textrm{\textbf{o}}B, C\textrm{\textbf{a}}B\vdash A\textrm{\textbf{o}}C\)
\(A\textrm{\textbf{e}}B, C\textrm{\textbf{i}}A, D\textrm{\textbf{a}}B\vdash C\textrm{\textbf{o}}D\)


Does every valid argument expressible in L\(_S\) have a proof in this system? The answer, as it turns out, is yes, but for now, you will just have to take my word for it.

title: 1  Argument, Validity & Proof
text:
This is Russell’s famous paradox, revealing the inconsistency of the formalization of naive set theory given by Frege (1893-1903) in his Grundgesetze der Arithmetik. In that work, Frege assumed that any property whatsoever—including the property of not being a member of itself—could be used in the formation of a well-defined set. For a detailed discussion of the various approaches adopted by modern formalizations of set theory to avoid Russell’s paradox, see Fraenkel, Levy, and Bar-Hillel (1973).↩︎
Because the subset relation is reflexive, it is sometimes denoted \(\subseteq\) (by analogy with the weak ordering relation \(\leq\)). On this notational scheme, it is the ‘proper’ subset relation (by analogy with the strict ordering relation \(<\)) that is denoted \(\subset\). The reason we do not adopt this notation is that the reflexive subset relation occurs far more frequently than its irreflexive counterpart. Symbolic parsimony thus dictates that it be awarded the unadorned marker.↩︎
The assumption that a sentence can take exactly one of two truth values is referred to as the principle of bivalence. Innocuous as it may seem, bivalence is perhaps the most contentious principle in all of logic. If you continue in your study of logic, you will encounter notions of argumentative validity that reject bivalence in favor of a ‘multivalent’ semantics in which sentences can be assigned one of many distinct truth values. You will also discover semantics in which a sentence may be assigned no truth value at all.↩︎
The branch of logic that studies the various modalities implicated in necessity claims is called modal logic. See Chapter ??? below.↩︎
By contrast, the intension of a common noun phrase is the meaning of the phrase, or that which determines its extension. Thus, for example, while ‘human’ and ‘featherless biped’ have the same extension since every human is a featherless biped and vice versa, they have different intensions since the two phrases differ in meaning. For a more detailed discussion of the distinction between extension and intension, see Carnap (1988), ch. 1.↩︎
The proof system introduced below was first proposed as a formalization of Aristotle’s theory of the syllogism by Corcoran (1972).↩︎
The names here given to the rules are taken from the following Latin poem created in the 13th century to help medieval logic students memorize the valid syllogistic forms:

Barbara, Celarent, Darii, Ferioque prima (first); Cesare, Camestres, Festino, Baroco secunda (second); Tertia (third) Darapti, Disamis, Datisi, Felapton, Bocardo, Ferison; Quarta (fourth) sunt Bramantip, Camenes, Dimaris, Fesapo, Fresison.

In the entry on “Traditional Logic” in Brody (2006) Glossary of Logical Terms, the key for the mnemonic employed in this poem is described as follows: “The three vowels respectively indicate the three constituent propositions of the syllogism as \(\textbf{a}\), \(\textbf{e}\), \(\textbf{i}\), or \(\textbf{o}\). For first-figure syllogisms the initial consonants are arbitrarily the first four consonants; for the other figures the initial consonants indicate to which of the first-figure syllogisms the syllogism in question may be reduced. Other consonants occurring in second-, third-, and fourth-figure mnemonics indicate the operation that must be performed on the proposition indicated by the preceding vowel in order to reduce the syllogism to a first-figure syllogism”.↩︎
Named after Frederic Brenton Fitch, who developed the notational scheme in the 1930s during his doctoral studies at Princeton under Alonzo Church.↩︎

title: 2  Propositional Logic
text:
2.1 Introduction
Propositional logic is the study of the inferential relations that obtain between compound propositions, such as:
\[
\textrm{Alice is a philosophy major \emph{and} Bob is majoring in economics.}
\]
\[
\textrm{\emph{Either} the train is late \emph{or} the schedule that is posted online is wrong}.
\]
\[
\textrm{\emph{If} the battery is not dead, \emph{then} the computer will turn on.}
\]
\[
\textrm{You are eligible to vote \emph{if and only if} you are a resident of the county}.
\]
Propositional logic forms the core of most other systems of logic utilized in philosophical analysis, including first-order quantificational logic and modal logic. It also provides the standard context in which the theory of probability is developed. It thus constitutes the first essential entry in a philosophy student’s logical toolkit. In this chapter, we will introduce a formal language in which to represent arguments of propositional logic, a semantic framework in which to assess their validity, and a proof system in which to carry out propositional reasoning.

title: 2  Propositional Logic
text:
2.2 The Syntax of Propositional Logic

2.2.1 Truth-Functional Operations
In natural language, many of the sentences that we assert have a compositional structure. They are, that is, built up from other sentences through the use of certain compound sentence forming operations. Let’s take a simple example. Consider the sentence:
\[
\textrm{Tomorrow it will rain.}
\]
From this sentence, we can construct a new sentence by adding to the verb the negating particle not:
\[
\textrm{Tomorrow it will \emph{not} rain.}
\]
Moreover, we can perform the very same operation on other sentences. For example, by negating the verb in the sentence:
\[
\textrm{Alice is a philosophy major.}
\]
we obtain a new sentence:
\[
\textrm{Alice is \emph{not} a philosophy major.}
\]
It is thus natural to think of the act of negating the verb as a general operation which takes us from one sentence to another, the latter being a compound sentence that is, in some way, made up of the former. We can also imagine compound sentence forming operations which act on more than one sentence. For example, given the two sentences:
\[
\textrm{Tomorrow it will rain.}
\]
and
\[
\textrm{Saturday will be sunny. }
\]
we can conjoin these sentences using the coordinating particle and to yield the new sentence:
\[
\textrm{Tomorrow it will rain \emph{and} Saturday will be sunny.}
\]
Here we have an operation which takes two sentences and returns a new compound sentence of which both of the original sentences are a part. Since it takes two sentences, we might call such an operation a binary operation, whereas negation, since it acts on only one sentence, would be unary.
How exactly is the sentence ‘Tomorrow it will not rain’, that results from performing the unary operation of negating the verb, related to the sentence ‘Tomorrow it will rain’ on which this operation is performed. The answer should be clear: the sentence which results from performing the operation of negation is one which is true just in case the sentence on which the operation was performed is false. This is likewise the case, for the sentence ‘Alice is not a philosophy major’ obtained by negating the sentence ‘Alice is a philosophy major’. So, we can think of negation as a general operation that takes a sentence to another sentence which is true just in case the original sentence is false.
What about the conjunction of two sentences? The conjunction of two sentences via the the coordinating particle and yields a sentence which is true just in case both of its component sentences are true. For example:
\[
\textrm{Tomorrow it will rain \emph{and} Saturday will be sunny.}
\]
is true just in case tomorrow there is rain and Saturday is sunny. Thus understood, both negation and conjunction, viewed as compound sentence forming operations, have something in common. Both operations result in sentences the truth value of which is entirely determined by the truth values of the component sentences on which these operations are performed. Such operations are called truth-functional operations since the truth or falsehood of their output is a function of the truth and falsehood of their inputs.1
There are lots of examples of non-truth-functional compound sentence forming operations in natural language. Take, for example, the result of combining the sentences:
\[
\textrm{The stock market went down.}
\]
and
\[
\textrm{There was a snowstorm in Boston.}
\]
via the subordinating conjunction ‘because’ to yield the sentence:
\[
\textrm{The stock market went down \emph{because} there was a snowstorm in Boston.}
\]
The truth or falsehood of this claim cannot be determined by that of the component sentences alone. In particular, if both sentences are true, the claim could be either true or false depending on whether the sentences stand in a suitable causal relationship to one another. Here are a few more examples of non-truth-functional compounds:
\[
\textrm{\emph{It could have been the case that} life developed on Mars.  }
\]
\[
\textrm{\emph{Lois Lane believes that} Superman is Clark Kent.}
\]
\[
\textrm{Caesar crossed the Rubicon \emph{after} he was declared emperor
of Rome.}
\]
In all of these cases, the truth value of the compound sentence is not determined by the truth values of its component parts.
In this chapter, we will be concerned to study the logic of truth-functional operations, like negation and conjunction. Other examples of truth-functional compounds include:
\[
\textrm{\emph{Either} the train is late \emph{or} the schedule that is posted online is wrong}.
\]
\[
\textrm{\emph{If} the battery is not dead, \emph{then} the computer will turn on.}
\]
\[
\textrm{You are eligible to vote \emph{if and only if} you are a resident of the county}.
\]
Our aim will be to develop tools to assess the validity of arguments involving such compound sentences—arguments such as:
\[
\frac{\textrm{Either James is sick at home or the store is closed
\hspace{3mm},\hspace{3mm} The store is not closed}}{\textrm{James is sick at home}}
\]
Just as before, we will study this logic in the context of a simplified formal language. This will allow us to avoid the unnecessary complexity of having to deal with natural language constructions. In English, for example, there are many ways to express the truth-functional operation of conjuntion, such as:
\[
\textrm{Tomorrow it will rain \emph{and} Saturday will be sunny.}
\]
\[
\textrm{Tomorrow it will rain, \emph{but} Saturday will be sunny.}
\]
\[
\textrm{\emph{While} tomorrow it will rain, Saturday will be sunny.}
\]
\[
\textrm{\emph{Even though} Tomorrow it will rain, Saturday will be sunny.}
\]
Moreover, in natural language, sentence forming operators that typically effect truth-functional operations do not always work in a uniform way. For instance, not every act of negating the verb has the effect of producing a sentence that is true just in case the original sentence is false. For example:
\[
\textrm{Thomas is not in a good mood.}
\]
does not appear to be true just in case:
\[
\textrm{Thomas is in a good mood.}
\]
is false. Both could perhaps be false, if Thomas happens to be in a state of resigned indifference.
To avoid such complications, we will formulate arguments of propositional logic in an artificial language devised for the express purpose of modeling the sort of arguments we are concerned to assess. We will call this formal language L\(_P\).2


2.2.2 The Formal Language L\(_P\)
To define the language L\(_P\), we begin by defining an alphabet for the language. This alphabet consists, first, of an arbitrarily large collection of symbols:
\[
  P, Q, R, \ldots
\]
referred to as atoms. In addition to the atoms, the alphabet also includes five special symbols:
\[
  \neg\hspace{0.05in},\hspace{0.05in} \wedge\hspace{0.05in},\hspace{0.05in}
  \vee\hspace{0.05in},\hspace{0.05in} \rightarrow,\hspace{0.05in} \leftrightarrow
\]
These five symbols are referred to collectively as propositional connectives, and, respectively, as the negation operator (\(\neg\)), the conjunction operator (\(\wedge\)), the disjunction operator (\(\vee\)), and the conditional operator (\(\rightarrow\)). The latter three connectives (i.e., \(\wedge, \vee\), and \(\rightarrow\)) are referred to as binary connectives, whereas the negation operator is referred to as a unary connective.
The intended meaning of the connectives is given by the following table:






Connective
Operation
English Operator




\(\neg\)
negation
It is not the case that \(\,\underline{\hspace{0.5cm}}\,\).


\(\wedge\)
conjunction
\(\cdots\) and \(\,\underline{\hspace{0.5cm}}\,\).


\(\vee\)
disjunction
\(\cdots\) or \(\,\underline{\hspace{0.5cm}}\,\) (or both).


\(\rightarrow\)
conditional
If \(\cdots\), then \(\,\underline{\hspace{0.5cm}}\,\).


\(\leftrightarrow\)
biconditional
\(\cdots\) if and only if \(\,\underline{\hspace{0.5cm}}\,\).





Figure 2.1: The intended meanings of the propositional connectives



Lastly, in addition to the atoms and connectives, the alphabet for L\(_P\) also includes the left and right parentheses:
\[
(\hspace{0.07in},\hspace{0.07in})
\]
As before, we will call an expression of the language any finite string of symbols taken from its alphabet. The sentences of the language will be constructed through the recursive application of the propositional connectives to the atoms. We will define the sentences in the following, somewhat awkward way:

Definition 2.1 A sentences of L\(_P\) is any expression that can be judged to be a sentence by appeal to the following facts:

Any expression consisting of a single atom is a sentence.
If \(\varphi\) is a sentence, then the expression \(\neg \varphi\) is a sentence.
If \(\varphi\) and \(\psi\) are sentences and \(\ast\) is a binary connective, then the expression \((\varphi\ast\psi)\) is a sentence.


This sort of definition is what is called in mathematics and logic an inductive definition. We will say more about how inductive definitions work in the next section. For now, we simply note that the following are all examples of sentences of \(L_{\mathbf{P}}\):

\[P\]
\[\neg Q\]
\[(R \leftrightarrow S)\]
\[\neg \neg (P \wedge Q)\]
\[((P\leftrightarrow\neg Q) \wedge(Q\vee R))\]
\[((Q\rightarrow(R\wedge(S \rightarrow P))) \leftrightarrow(P\rightarrow\neg Q))\]

Note that, unlike the sentences of L\(_S\), the sentences of the formal language L\(_P\) can grow to be arbitrarily complex.
We refer to those sentences consisting of a single atom as atomic sentences. The sentence \(\neg \varphi\) is referred to as the negation of \(\varphi\). The sentence \((\varphi\wedge\psi)\) is referred to as the conjunction with left conjunct \(\varphi\) and right conjunct \(\psi\), or simply the conjunction of \(\varphi\) and \(\psi\). The sentence \((\varphi\vee\psi)\) is referred to as the disjunction with left disjunct \(\varphi\) and right disjunct \(\psi\), or simply the disjunction of \(\varphi\) and \(\psi\). The sentence \((\varphi\rightarrow\psi)\) is referred to as the conditional with antecedent \(\varphi\) and consequent \(\psi\). The sentence \((\varphi\leftrightarrow\psi)\) is referred to as the biconditional with left term \(\varphi\) and right term \(\psi\). Conjunctions, disjunctions, conditionals, and biconditionals are referred to, collectively, as binary compounds.

Exercise 2.1 Translate the following arguments into the language L\(_P\), indicating the meaning of all the atoms appearing in your translation.
(a). The professor is speaking and either the students are taking notes or they are recording the lecture.
(b). The meeting will be held if and only if the keynote speaker does not miss her flight.
(c). Either the file is not accessible or the password is incorrect.
(d). If the library is open and I have finished studying, then I will either go there or stay at home and read.
(e). If it is warm, we will sit outside, but if not, we will stay inside.



2.2.3 Inductive Definitions
Let’s go back and look more closely at our definition of a sentence of L\(_P\):

Definition 2.2 A sentences of L\(_P\) is any expression that can be judged to be a sentence by appeal to the following facts:

Any expression consisting of a single atom is a sentence.
If \(\varphi\) is a sentence, then the expression \(\neg \varphi\) is a sentence.
If \(\varphi\) and \(\psi\) are sentences and \(\ast\) is a binary connective, then the expression \((\varphi\ast\psi)\) is a sentence.


Consider the expression:
\[ \neg (P \wedge Q)\]
Is this expression a sentence, according to the above definition? The definition says that the expression is a sentence provided it can be judged to be so on the bases of clauses 1–3. Let’s first look at clause 2. This clause states that if \(\varphi\) is a sentence, then \(\neg\varphi\) is a sentences as well. So, in the case of the expression \(\neg(P\wedge Q)\), it suffices to show that this is a sentence to show that \((P\wedge Q)\) is a sentence. How can we confirm that this is true? Have a look at clause 3. This clause says that if \(\varphi\) and \(\psi\) are sentences, then \((\varphi\ast\psi)\) is also a sentence, where \(\ast\) is any binary connective. It follows from this that the expression \((P\wedge Q)\) is a sentence if both \(P\) and \(Q\) are sentences. How can we confirm that this is true? Look at clause 1. This clause says that any expression consisting of a single atom is a sentence, so since \(P\) and \(Q\) are both expressions of this sort, they are both sentences.
So, it turns out, \(\neg(P\wedge Q)\) is a sentence of L\(_P\). How do we know this? Because \(P\) and \(Q\) are both sentences (by clause 1), and so \((P\wedge Q)\) is a sentence (by clause 3), and so \(\neg(P\wedge Q)\) is a sentence (by clause 2).
A definition that works like this is called an inductive definition. Inductive definitions are very common in mathematics, logic, computer science, and linguistics—any field in which the items to be studied exhibit a recursive compositional structure, like the sentences of a language. Inductive definitions take the following form: they first tell us, flatly, that certain items fall under the defined term. They then make one or more claims to the effect that if certain items fall under that term, then certain other items fall under that term as well. In this way, inductive definitions specify a kind of repeatable, or recursive, procedure by which new items falling under the defined term can be generated or constructed from older ones, and they stipulate that an object belongs to defined class of items just in case it can be constructed in this way. The clause specifying the starting items from which the construction can proceed is called the base clause of the inductive definition, the rest of the clauses that tell us how to construct new items from old ones, are called the inductive clauses. So, in the above definition of L\(_P\), clause 1 is the base clause and clauses 2 and 3 the inductive ones.
Let’s consider another example of an inductive definition. Suppose we want to give an inductive definition of the natural numbers, that is, the numbers belonging to the set:
\[\{1, 2, 3, \ldots\}\]
One way of thinking about how a number gets into this set is as follows: first, we stipulate that \(1\) is in the set. Then, we posit that if a number, say \(n\), is already in the set, then \(n+1\) goes into the set as well. We can turn this idea into an inductive definition as follows:

Definition 2.3 A natural number is any number that can be judged to be a natural number by appeal to the following facts:

\(1\) is a natural number.
If \(n\) is a natural number, then \(n+1\) is a natural number.


It is easy to see that a number like \(17\) is a natural number according to this definition. For, \(1\) is a natural number (by clause 1), and so \(2\) is a natural number (by clause 2), and so \(3\) is a natural number (by clause 2), and so … \(17\) is a natural number (by clause 2).
Note that a single term or notion may have several different inductive definitions. For example, here are two more inductive definitions of the notion of a natural number. The first is fairly obvious, the second less so:

Definition 2.4 A natural number is any number that can be judged to be a natural number by appeal to the following facts:

\(1\) is a natural number.
If \(1, 2, \ldots, n\) are all natural numbers, then \(n+1\) is a natural number.



Definition 2.5 A natural number is any number that can be judged to be a natural number by appeal to the following facts:

Every number that is either \(1\) or a prime is a natural number.
If \(n\) and \(m\) are natural numbers, then \(n\times m\) is a natural number.


One of the main advantages of inductive definitions is that such definitions can provide a straightforward means of proving that a certain property is possessed by every item belonging to the defined class. In particular, if an item is defined inductively, then it suffices to show that:

Every item mentioned in the base clause of the definition has the property in question.
The property is preserved under each of the inductive clauses of the definition. In other words, if all items to which the inductive clause is applied possess the property in question, then any new items which fall under the term in accordance with this clause have that property as well.

A proof that proceeds in this way is called a proof by induction, or an inductive proof—not to be confused with an inductive argument in the sense of David Hume! Here is an example of an inductive proof, using the above definition of a natural number.

Theorem 2.1 For any natural number \(n\):
\[ 1+2+\cdots+n = \frac{n(n+1)}{2} \]

Proof. We first observe that the claim holds for \(n=1\):
\[
1 = \frac{1(2)}{2}
\]
Now, suppose that the claim holds for the natural number \(n\). Then:
\[
\begin{align}
        1 + 2 + \cdots + n + (n + 1) & = \frac{n(n + 1)}{2} + (n + 1)\quad \\
                                     & = \frac{n(n + 1) + 2(n + 1)}{2}\:   \\
                                     & = \frac{(n + 1)(n + 2)}{2}
\end{align}
\]
So the claim also holds for \(n+1\). This completes the proof.


While an inductive proof such as this may seem like magic, much of the challenge of supplying inductive proofs consists in identifying suitable inductive definitions for the defined class of items.

Exercise 2.2 Using Definition 2.4, give a proof by induction that every natural number is either equal to 1 or can be expressed as a product of primes.


Exercise 2.3 The various ways of making change for a dollar using pennies, nickels, dimes, and quarters may be represented by the members of the following set of 4-tuples of numbers:
\[
D = \{(p,n,d,q): (1\times p) + (5\times n) + (10\times d) + (25\times q) = 100\}
\]
The members of this set can be given the following inductive definition:

\((0,0,0,4)\in D\).
If \((p,n,d,q)\in D\) and \(q> 0\), then \((p,n+1,d+2,q-1)\in D\).
If \((p,n,d,q)\in D\) and \(q>1\), then \((p, n, d+5, q-2)\in D\).
If \((p,n,d,q)\in D\) and \(d> 0\), then \((p,n+2,d-1,q)\in D\).
If \((p,n,d,q)\in D\) and \(n> 0\), then \((p+5, n-1, d, q)\in D\).

Using this inductive definition, show that the following two claims are true:
(a). There is no way to make change for a dollar from exactly five coins.
(b). There is no way to make change for a dollar from an assortment of coins in which the total number of non-dimes (i.e., pennies, nickels, and quarters) is odd.

One useful way of conceptualizing the items picked out by an inductive definition is by means of a ‘formation tree’. This is a tree structure, each node of which is labeled by an item falling under the defined term. The direct descendants of any given node in the tree are items which generate their parent node in accordance with one of the inductive clauses of the definition, and the root nodes of the tree (the nodes with no direct descendants) are items falling under the term in accordance with the base clause of the definition.
So, for example, a formation tree for the sentence \(\neg(P\wedge Q)\) is as follows:





Here is a formation tree for the sentence \(((P\leftrightarrow\neg Q)\wedge(Q\vee R))\):





While it is true that every object belonging to an inductively defined set has a formation tree, it need not be the case that every object in the set has a unique formation tree. Suppose for example, we were to omit parentheses from the alphabet of L\(_P\) and define the sentences of L\(_P\) as follows:

Definition 2.6 A sentences of L\(_P\) is any expression that can be judged to be a sentence by appeal to the following facts:

Any expression consisting of a single atom is a sentence.
If \(\varphi\) is a sentence, then the expression \(\neg \varphi\) is a sentence.
If \(\varphi\) and \(\psi\) are sentences and \(\ast\) is a binary connective, then the expression \(\varphi\ast\psi\) is a sentence.


In accordance with this definition, the expression \(P \wedge Q \vee R\) is a sentence of the language. Note, however, that the sentence has two distinct formation trees:





and:





We will shortly see why it is important to preclude the possibility of this sort of syntactic ambiguity in our language, by enforcing a strict order of operations.

title: 2  Propositional Logic
section: 2.3 The Meaning of the Propositional Connectives
text:
2.3 The Meaning of the Propositional Connectives

2.3.1 Truth Tables
Our next task is to explain what makes an argument in the language L\(_P\) a valid argument. Just as before, the idea of argumentative validity will be expressed in terms of necessary truth preservation. An argument is valid just in case the truth of the conclusion follows necessarily from that of the premises. Moreover, the relevant notion of necessity will still be made out in semantic terms. An argument is valid just in case there is no interpretation of the non-logical elements of the language under which the premises are true and the conclusion false.
What are the non-logical elements of the language L\(_P\)? In this case, they are the atoms. These, naturally, are meant to stand for the sort of simple propositions out of which compound propositions are constructed. So, an argument is valid just in case the truth of the conclusion follows from that of the premises, no matter what propositions the atoms stand for.
But what exactly is a proposition? From the perspective of the general philosophy of language, this, of course, is a complicated question. But from the point of view of truth-functional propositional logic, it admits of a fairly straightforward answer. In the context of logic, what is important about an interpretation of a language is that it serves to underwrite a specific truth valuation of the sentences of the language. Since we are dealing exclusively with truth-functional operations, the truth values of compound sentences will be fully determined by the truth values of the component sentences out of which they are formed. In this way, the truth values of all the sentences of the language will supervene on the truth values of the atomic ones.
So, while the atomic sentences do represent simple propositions, the only feature of these propositions that is semantically relevant in the sense that it contributes to assessing the truth values of others sentences in the language, is their own truth or falsehood. Thus, in this context, we may think of a simple proposition as nothing more than the bearer of a truth value, and the possible semantic values of the atoms as the possible truth values of the propositions they express. The only assumptions we will make about these simple propositions are (1) that each is logically contingent in the sense that each is a proposition that may be either true or false; and (2) that they are logically independent of each other in the sense that the truth or falsehood of any one such proposition imposes no constraints on the truth or falsehood of any other.
This means that, we will include among our interpretations of the language all possible assignments of truth values to the atoms. We thus arrive at the following definition:

Definition 2.7 An interpretation of the language L\(_P\) is a function \(\sigma\) which assigns to each of the atoms a truth value, i.e., an object from the set \(\{\mathbf{T},
  \mathbf{F}\}\).

To complete our specification of the semantics for the language, we must explain how a given interpretation \(\sigma\) determines a unique truth valuation \(v^\sigma\) for the language. In doing so, we will, in effect, be explaining the meanings of the logical elements of the syntax, that is, the propositional connectives.
For the atomic sentences, the truth value of a sentence is given directly by the interpretation as follows:
(Atomic Sentences): If \(\varphi\) is an atom, then \(v^\sigma(\varphi)=\sigma(\varphi)\).3
To assign truth values to compound sentences, we must introduce additional semantic rules, specifying the meaning of the propositional connectives. We do so with the aim of interpreting the propositional connectives as effecting roughly the same semantic operations as the ordinary English locutions to which they correspond in Figure 2.1.
Let’s begin with negation. We have already said that the truth-functional operation effected by negation is that of transforming one sentence into another sentence that is true just in case the original sentence is false. This can be captured by the following rule:
(Negation): For any sentence \(\varphi\): \(v^\sigma(\neg\varphi)=\mathbf{T}\) iff \(v^\sigma(\varphi)=\mathbf{F}\).
Notice the ‘iff’ here. Since we are still assuming that every sentence is either true or false, and that no sentence is both, it follows from our rule for negation that if \(v^\sigma(\varphi)=\mathbf{T}\), then \(v^\sigma(\neg\varphi)=\mathbf{F}\).
Now, let’s consider conjunction. Again, as we’ve already said the truth-functional operation of conjunction takes two sentences and returns a new sentence which is true iff both of its component sentences—or conjuncts—are true. We can describe this operation as follows:
(Conjunction): For any sentences \(\varphi\) and \(\psi\): \(v^\sigma((\varphi\wedge\psi))=\mathbf{T}\) iff \(v^\sigma(\varphi)=\mathbf{T}\) and \(v^\sigma(\psi)=\mathbf{T}\).
With the rules for negation and conjunction in place, we can now consider the truth conditions for more complicated compound sentences, such as \(\neg(P\wedge
\neg Q)\). How does the truth value of this sentence depend upon the truth values of the atoms \(P\) and \(Q\)? By the rule for negation, the sentence \(\neg(P\wedge\neg Q)\) is true iff the sentence \((P\wedge\neg Q)\) is false. By the rule for conjunction, \((P\wedge\neg Q)\) is false iff either \(P\) is false or \(\neg Q\) is false (or both). Since \(\neg Q\) is false iff \(Q\) is true, the whole sentence \(\neg(P\wedge\neg Q)\) is true iff either \(P\) is false or \(Q\) is true (or both).
One particularly convenient format for expressing the truth conditions for compound sentences is called a truth table. In a truth table, each of the atoms appearing in the sentence is assigned a separate column and each row corresponds to a different possible assignment of truth values to these atoms. The final column in the table then specifies the truth values of the compound sentence in question for each interpretation of the atoms. For example, a truth table for the sentence \(\neg(P\wedge\neg Q)\) is as follows:



\(P\)
\(Q\)
\(\neg(P\wedge\neg Q)\)




\(\mathbf{T}\)
\(\mathbf{T}\)
\(\mathbf{T}\)


\(\mathbf{T}\)
\(\mathbf{F}\)
\(\mathbf{F}\)


\(\mathbf{F}\)
\(\mathbf{T}\)
\(\mathbf{T}\)


\(\mathbf{F}\)
\(\mathbf{F}\)
\(\mathbf{T}\)



Note that this truth table has four rows. That is because the sentence in question contains two atoms. Since each atom can take one of two truth values, in order to countenance all possible interpretations, we need \(2\times 2 = 4\) rows. If we considered a sentence containing three distinct atoms, we would need a truth table with \(2 \times 2\times 2 = 8\) rows. In general, a sentence with \(n\) distinct atoms requires a truth table with \(2^n\) rows.
When populating the initial columns in a truth table, it is important to ensure that all possible interpretations of the atoms are countenanced. The most straightforward way to ensure that this is the case is to enumerate the truth values of the atoms in accordance with the following systematic procedure: in the first column, fill in the first half of the rows with \(\mathbf{T}\) and the second half with \(\mathbf{F}\). In the second column, fill in the first quarter of the rows with \(\mathbf{T}\), the second quarter with \(\mathbf{F}\), the third quarter with \(\mathbf{T}\), and the last quarter with \(\mathbf{F}\). Continue in this way, writing down alternating blocks of \(\mathbf{T}\) and \(\mathbf{F}\) and at each new column cutting the size of the blocks in half, until you reach the last column for an atom in which \(\mathbf{T}\) and \(\mathbf{F}\) alternate row by row.


2.3.2 Logical Equivalence
Now, let’s return to our specification of the meanings of the propositional connectives. Disjunction corresponds to the English language operation ‘either … or —’. Now, in natural language there exists a certain ambiguity in how we interpret such expressions. Sometimes we interpret these expressions ‘exclusively’ as meaning that one of the two component sentences is true, but not both. For instance:
Either you may have coffee or you may have tea.
In other cases, we interpret disjunctions ‘inclusively’ as meaning that at least one, and possibly both, of the component sentences are true, as in:
Either the train is late or the schedule posted online is wrong.
In specifying the meaning of the disjunction operator in the language L\(_P\), we will assume that it signifies inclusive disjunction, and hence that a disjunction is true just in case either (or both) of its component sentences—or, disjuncts—is true:
(Disjunction): For any sentences \(\varphi\) and \(\psi\): \(v^\sigma((\varphi\wedge\psi))=\mathbf{T}\) iff \(v^\sigma(\varphi)=\mathbf{T}\) or \(v^\sigma(\psi)=\mathbf{T}\) (or both).
The truth table for the sentence \(P\vee Q\) is as follows:



\(P\)
\(Q\)
\(P\vee Q\)




\(\mathbf{T}\)
\(\mathbf{T}\)
\(\mathbf{T}\)


\(\mathbf{T}\)
\(\mathbf{F}\)
\(\mathbf{T}\)


\(\mathbf{F}\)
\(\mathbf{T}\)
\(\mathbf{T}\)


\(\mathbf{F}\)
\(\mathbf{F}\)
\(\mathbf{F}\)




Exercise 2.4 Let \(\varphi\) and \(\psi\) be any sentences of L\(_P\). Write down a sentence of L\(_P\) that expresses the exclusive disjunction of \(\varphi\) and \(\psi\). Confirm this by giving a truth table.

We are now in a position to understand why we needed to introduce parentheses into our language of propositional logic. Suppose we omitted the parentheses and allowed for unparenthesized sentences such as \(P\wedge Q\vee R\). As we observed in Section 2.2.3, this sentence would have two distinct formation trees. What the semantic rules for conjunction and disjunction tell us is, in effect, how truth values assigned to the terminal nodes of such a tree, labeled by atoms, are transmitted up through the tree to the compound sentence labeling the root node of the tree, in which those atoms figure. It would, of course, be a bad thing if some assignment of truth values to the atoms, transmits to the root node of one of the formation trees for a sentence a truth value that is different from that which it transmits to the root node of another formation tree for that same sentence. For in this case, the rules would generate an inconsistency, implying that the sentence is both true and false.
It turns out that this sort of incoherency would be possible if we omitted parentheses from the language. For example, let \(\sigma(P)=\mathbf{F}\), \(\sigma(Q)=\mathbf{T}\), and \(\sigma(R)=\mathbf{T}\). Then, transmitting these values up through the two formation trees for the sentence \(P\wedge Q\vee R\) in accordance with the rules for conjunction and disjunction results in different truth values being assigned to this sentence. Following one tree it is assigned the value false:





But following the other it is assigned the value true:





To avoid this sort of semantic incoherence, we need our language to distinguish between these two sentences. This is why we introduced parentheses into our language. By means of parentheses, we enforce a strict ‘order of operations’ which ensures that no sentence admits of multiple distinct formation trees. In this way, we disambiguate the sentence \(P\wedge Q\vee R\), associating its first formation tree with the sentence \((P\wedge(Q\vee R))\) and its second formation tree with the sentence \(((P\wedge Q)\vee R)\).
Now, that we have better understood the need for parentheses, we can be a bit more lax in our use of parentheses for the sake of readability. In particular, when writing sentences, we will omit the outermost pair of parentheses. We will also assume an order of operations for the propositional connectives that will allow us to express sentences using fewer parentheses than are strictly required by the grammatical rules of L\(_P\). In accordance with this order of operations, negations are to be parenthesized first, in as narrow a manner as possible, then conjunctions and disjunctions, then conditionals and, finally, biconditionals. Thus, for example, to express the sentence: \[ ((\neg P\wedge Q)\rightarrow
\neg R) \] we may write: \[ \neg P \wedge Q \rightarrow\neg R \]

Exercise 2.5 If we wish to omit the parentheses from our language in a manner which avoids semantic ambiguity, we can adopt what is called a prefix (rather than the standard infix) notation for expressing sentential compounds. This prefix notation is sometimes referred to as Polish notation.
In Polish notation, we define the sentences of the language inductively as follows:

Any expression consisting of a single atom is an sentence.
If \(\varphi\) is a sentence then \(\neg \varphi\) is a sentence.
If \(\varphi\) and \(\psi\) are sentences and \(*\) is a binary connective, then the expression \(\ast \varphi
\psi\) is a sentence.

For example, in Polish notation, the sentence with the same formation tree as \((P\wedge (Q\vee R))\) is written:
\[ \wedge P\!\vee\! Q R \]
while the sentence with the same formation tree as \(((P\wedge Q)\vee R)\) is written:
\[ \vee \!\wedge\! P Q R \]
Rewrite the sentences of L\(_P\) listed at the end of Section 2.2.2 in Polish notation.

Let’s now consider the conditional operator. This operator is meant to model the English language construction ‘if …, then —’. But of all the connectives, it is however, perhaps the farthest removed from its natural language counterpart. This is because conditional constructions are used to express a variety of different relationships between sentences, most of which are not truth-functional. For example:
\[
\textrm{If the match is struck, then it will light.}
\]
This conditional expresses a causal relationship, and so, as we have already noted, its truth value does not depend on the truth value of its component sentences—referred to as its antecedent and consequent, respectively.
So, how should we think of a conditional in strictly truth-functional terms? To begin with, we may assume that a conditional is true if both its antecedent and consequent are true. Moreover, we may assume that it is false if its antecedent is true and its consequent false. The harder cases to handle are those in which the antecedent is false. What truth value should we assign to the conditional in these cases? We will adopt the convention, odd as it may seem, that a conditional with a false antecedent is true. As we will see later, there is a proof theoretic rationale for this policy.
So, the semantic rule for conditionals is as follows:
(Conditional): For any sentences \(\varphi\) and \(\psi\): \(v^\sigma(\varphi\rightarrow\psi)=\mathbf{F}\) iff \(v^\sigma(\varphi)=\mathbf{T}\) and \(v^\sigma(\psi)=\mathbf{F}\).
The truth table for \(P\rightarrow Q\) is as follows:



\(P\)
\(Q\)
\(P\rightarrow Q\)




\(\mathbf{T}\)
\(\mathbf{T}\)
\(\mathbf{T}\)


\(\mathbf{T}\)
\(\mathbf{F}\)
\(\mathbf{F}\)


\(\mathbf{F}\)
\(\mathbf{T}\)
\(\mathbf{T}\)


\(\mathbf{F}\)
\(\mathbf{F}\)
\(\mathbf{T}\)



Here is a fact about the conditional that is worth noting:
\[\Gamma, \varphi\models\psi\textrm{ iff }\Gamma\models\varphi\rightarrow\psi\]
The claim \(\Gamma,\varphi\models\psi\) is true iff there is no interpretation under which every sentence in \(\Gamma\cup\{\varphi\}\) is true and \(\psi\) is false. This just means that there is no interpretation under which every sentence in \(\Gamma\) is true, \(\varphi\) is true, and \(\psi\) is false. But, given the meaning of the conditional, this is equivalent to the claim that there is no interpretation under which every sentence in \(\Gamma\) is true and \(\varphi\rightarrow\psi\) is false, i.e., \(\Gamma\models\varphi\rightarrow\psi\).
Notice that the truth conditions for the conditional \(P\rightarrow Q\) are exactly the same as the truth conditions for the sentence \(\neg(P\wedge\neg Q)\). Both are true just in case either \(P\) is false or \(Q\) is true (or both). This highlights the fact that the syntax of the language L\(_P\) now greatly exceeds its expressive capacity. That is, in the language, there are many distinct ways of saying the same thing.
When two sentences have exactly the same truth conditions, we say that they are logically equivalent.

Definition 2.8 Two sentences \(\varphi\) and \(\psi\) are logically equivalent, written \(\varphi\equiv
\psi\), if, for any interpretation \(\sigma\), \(v^\sigma(\varphi)=v^\sigma(\psi)\).

Two other important semantic notions are those of logical truth and logical falsehood:

Definition 2.9 A sentence \(\varphi\) is a logical truth if, for every interpretation \(\sigma\): \(v^\sigma(\varphi)=\mathbf{T}\). A sentence \(\varphi\) is a logical falsehood if, for every interpretation \(\sigma\): \(v^\sigma(\varphi)=\mathbf{F}\).

In the context of propositional logic, logical truths are sometimes referred to as tautologies and logical falsehoods as contradictions. In fact, our final connective, the biconditional, allows us to express logical equivalence in terms of logical truth. The rule for the biconditional is as follows:
(Biconditional): For any sentences \(\varphi\) and \(\psi\): \(v^\sigma(\varphi\leftrightarrow\psi)=\mathbf{T}\) iff \(v^\sigma(\varphi)=v^\sigma(\psi)\).
Convince yourself that, for any sentences \(\varphi\) and \(\psi\), \(\varphi\equiv\psi\) iff the sentence \(\varphi\leftrightarrow\psi\) is a tautology.

Exercise 2.6 Use truth tables to establish the following claims, where \(\varphi\), \(\psi\), and \(\chi\) are any sentences:

\(\neg(\varphi\wedge\psi) \equiv \neg\varphi\vee\neg\psi\)
\((\varphi\wedge\neg\varphi)\vee\psi\equiv \psi\)
\(\neg\neg\varphi\equiv \varphi\)
\(\varphi\rightarrow\psi\equiv\neg\psi\rightarrow\neg\varphi\)
\(\varphi\rightarrow(\psi\rightarrow\chi)\equiv (\varphi\wedge\psi)\rightarrow\chi\)



Exercise 2.7 Let \(\varphi\) be a sentence in which the only connectives are \(\neg\), \(\wedge\), and \(\vee\). The dual of \(\varphi\) is the sentence which results from replacing every occurrence of the connective \(\wedge\) in \(\varphi\) with the connective \(\vee\), and vice versa. For example, the dual of the sentence:
\[ (P\wedge Q)\vee R \]
is the sentence:
\[ (P\vee Q)\wedge R\]
If two sentences \(\varphi\) and \(\psi\) are logically equivalent, then so are their duals. Illustrate this by confirming (using truth tables) that the following equivalences are still true when the sentences are replaced with their duals:

\(\neg(P\wedge Q)\equiv \neg P\vee\neg Q\)
\(P\wedge(Q\vee R)\equiv (P\wedge Q)\vee (P\wedge R)\)

A sentence is self-dual if it is logically equivalent to its own dual.

Show that the sentence:

\[ (P\wedge Q)\vee(Q\wedge R)\vee (P\wedge R) \]
is self-dual. Describe what this sentence means in way that explains why it is self-dual.



2.3.3 Truth-Functional Completeness
When we set up the formal language L\(_P\), we posited five propositional connectives, \(\neg\), \(\wedge\), \(\vee\), \(\rightarrow\), and \(\leftrightarrow\). But why did we stop there? Is there something special about this particular set of connectives? Why did we not add more connectives to make our language even more expressive?
When we talk about the expressive power of the language, what we mean to ask is whether or not our language is capable of capturing various truth-functional relationships that may obtain between the atoms. Truth-functional relationships are, after all, the only kind of relationship that we are interested in. Here is one way of thinking about this question: consider a truth table in which we fill in the final column in a totally haphazard way, randomly assigning \(\mathbf{T}\)’s and \(\mathbf{F}\)’s to the rows. The resulting truth table will express some, possibly complex, truth-functional relationship between the atoms appearing in the initial columns. The question is, are we able to express that relationship by means of a sentence of the language L\(_P\). Or are there some truth-functional relationships for which we would need to add to the language additional propositional connectives?
As we shall see, the answer to this last question is no. Our current set of connectives is truth-functionally complete, in the sense that it is able to express any truth-functional relationship whatsoever. Moreover, it is even redundant in the sense that we could omit certain connectives from the language without any loss of expressive power.

Is there a sentence of L\(_P\) that corresponds to this truth table? Note that such a sentence should be \(\mathbf{T}\) iff its arguments are those given in either the first, third, fifth, or sixth rows of the table. So, we might start by formulating our desired sentence as a disjunction of these four possibilities:
\[
  Row_1 \vee Row_3 \vee Row_5 \vee Row_6
\]
Now, to be in row 1 means that \(P, Q,\) and \(R\) are all true. So, the sentence \(Row_1\) can be formulated by means of the following conjunction:
\[
  P\wedge Q\wedge R
\]
\(Row_3\) is a sentence asserting that \(P\) is true, \(Q\) is false, and $ R$ is true. This can be formulated as follows:
\[
  P\wedge\neg Q\wedge R

Formulating the sentences \(Row_5\) and \(Row_6\) in similar fashion, we can express the desired sentence corresponding to the above truth table by the following disjunction:
\[
  (P\wedge Q\wedge R) \vee (P\wedge\neg  Q\wedge R) \vee (\neg P\wedge Q\wedge R)
  \vee (\neg P\wedge Q\wedge\neg R)
\]
With only one exception, this technique is fully generalizable, and can be used to construct a sentence using only \(\neg,\wedge,\) and \(\vee\) which expresses any truth function with any number of arguments. The one case that it doesn’t cover is the degenerate case in which the truth function in question always returns the truth value \(\mathbf{F}\). But this truth function is expressible by the sentence \(P\wedge\neg P\). So, it appears that the set of connectives \(\neg,\wedge,\vee\) is all that we need complete.
In fact, we can push things a little further. Given the following equivalence:
\[ \varphi\wedge\psi \equiv \neg\varphi\vee\neg\psi \]
every conjunction can be replaced with a sentence that uses only \(\neg\) and \(\vee\). So these two connectives alone would suffice.
Can we make do with just one connective? The answer, it turns out, is yes! Let \(\uparrow\) be the binary propositional connective, whose corresponding truth function is given by the truth table:

2.3.4 Validity
Let us gather all of our semantic rules together. For any sentences, \(\varphi\) and \(\psi\):
(Atomic Sentences): If \(\varphi\) is an atom, then \(v^\sigma(\varphi)=\sigma(\varphi)\).
(Negation): \(v^\sigma(\neg\varphi)=\mathbf{T}\) iff \(v^\sigma(\varphi)=\mathbf{F}\).
(Conjunction): \(v^\sigma((\varphi\wedge\psi))=\mathbf{T}\) iff \(v^\sigma(\varphi)=\mathbf{T}\) and \(v^\sigma(\psi)=\mathbf{T}\).
(Disjunction): \(v^\sigma((\varphi\wedge\psi))=\mathbf{T}\) iff \(v^\sigma(\varphi)=\mathbf{T}\) or \(v^\sigma(\psi)=\mathbf{T}\).
(Conditional): \(v^\sigma(\varphi\rightarrow\psi)=\mathbf{F}\) iff \(v^\sigma(\varphi)=\mathbf{T}\) and \(v^\sigma(\psi)=\mathbf{F}\).
(Biconditional): \(v^\sigma(\varphi\leftrightarrow\psi)=\mathbf{T}\) iff \(v^\sigma(\varphi)=v^\sigma(\psi)\).
Taken together, these rules specify the meanings of the propositional connectives, and they allow us to compute the truth or falsehood of any sentence in the language L\(_P\) on a given interpretation of the language. This is enough to supply us with a precise standard for argumentative validity.
Consider, for example, the argument:

\hspace{3mm},\hspace{3mm} The store is not closed}}{\textrm{James is sick at home}}
\]

\(Q\): The store is closed
we can translate this argument into the language L\(_P\) as follows:

References
